Mira Murati, former chief technology officer of OpenAI, surprised Silicon Valley when she[resigned from the unicorn in September 2024](https://fortune.com/2024/09/26/mira-murati-openai-cto-departure-women/): She had been the driving force behind ChatGPT, the revolutionary AI model. Six months later,[Murati launched Thinking Machines Lab](https://fortune.com/2025/02/18/former-openai-cto-mira-murati-finally-unveils-her-thinking-machines-lab-startup-and-a-leadership-team-stacked-with-former-openai-colleagues/), an AI startup that’s generating buzz even though it’s still in stealth mode. But while details about the project remain thin, the 36-year-old Murati’s clout in the AI sector is clear: In mid-May,[she was reportedly close to landing $2 billion in what would represent the largest seed round in tech history](https://fortune.com/2025/04/10/mira-murati-2-billion-seed-raise-ai-boom-economic-chaos/), valuing Thinking Machines at $10 billion.

# Mira Murati: The woman behind ChatGPT and AI's future

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[Shivani Berry](https://www.linkedin.com/in/shivaniberry?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) Shivani Berry is an Influencer

3mo

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fshivaniberry_chatgpt-has-800-million-users-without-her-activity-7368659029352448000-tpt1&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

ChatGPT has 800 million users. Without her it wouldn’t exist. She is the most influential woman in AI.
Born in Albania in 1988, Mira Murati grew up during political upheaval.
At 16, she won a scholarship to Pearson College UWC in Canada, where critical thinking and global citizenship became her compass.
She then pursued a dual academic path: a Bachelor of Arts at Colby College in 2011 and a Bachelor of Engineering from Dartmouth’s Thayer School in 2012.
She did an internship at Goldman Sachs and moved to [Zodiac Aerospace](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/zodiac-aerospace?trk=public_post-text).
Then came [Tesla](https://www.linkedin.com/company/tesla-motors?trk=public_post-text), where she worked on the Model X and Autopilot as a senior product manager.
But her defining chapter began in 2018 when she joined OpenAI as Vice President of Applied AI and Partnerships.
By 2022, she was promoted to CTO.
Under her leadership, the team built ChatGPT, DALL·E, Codex, and Sora.
She even stepped in as interim CEO during OpenAI’s leadership crisis.
But Mira wasn’t just shipping products.
She was shaping how AI should be built.
She spoke openly about bias, transparency, and safety. “AI should be aligned with human intention and ultimately in the service of humanity.”
In 2024, she walked away from [OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text).
And in early 2025, she founded Thinking Machines Labs.
Her vision inspired instant belief. The company raised a record-breaking $2 billion seed round at a $12 billion valuation.
And when Meta tried to poach her team with offers worth up to $1 billion each, not a single person left.
She has received recognitions like:
• Time’s 100 Most Influential People in AI (2024).
• Fortune’s Most Powerful Women in Business (2023).
• Honorary doctorate from Dartmouth.
Mira Murati didn’t just help build ChatGPT. She built conviction, loyalty, and a new movement in AI.
A fiery trailblazer. 🔥
Mira is proof that in STEM (and in leadership) - values are just as powerful as vision.

## We are thrilled to share the news that Mustafa Suleyman is joining Greylock as a Venture Partner. There are few people who are as visionary, knowledgeable, and connected across the vast artificial intelligence landscape as Mustafa. At Greylock, Mustafa will spend his time advising early-stage companies and investing in promising startups in the AI space.
Mustafa joins us from Google, where he was Vice President of AI Product Management and AI Policy. Before that he co-founded DeepMind, the world’s leading artificial intelligence company, which was acquired by Google in 2014 for $650M.
I’ve known Mustafa personally for more than a decade. We first met at a pub in London. While I have some fun memories of that night (including trying my first “toad in the hole”), what struck me most was our inspiring and provocative discussion on the power and potential of AI to help solve some of humanity’s most urgent challenges. It marked the beginning of a deep and lasting friendship between Mustafa and I.
There’s no doubt that AI is one of the most transformative technologies of our time. Mustafa has been at the forefront of some of the most exciting advances in this space. During his time at DeepMind, Mustafa led teams inventing and deploying cutting edge AI systems to more accurately detect breast cancer in mammograms, to diagnose 50 different eye diseases in OCT scans, and to control Google’s multi-billion dollar data centers to optimize energy consumption. He also worked with many teams across Google to apply the latest AI techniques in Android, Hardware, Play, and Cloud.
Over the years DeepMind achieved many groundbreaking contributions to the field of AI research and applications. Most notably DeepMind developed AlphaGo, an AI system that beat the world’s strongest Go player in a now legendary multi-day competition broadcast live and watched by millions of people across the world.
Moreover, Mustafa and I share a common love of philosophy, which drives how both of us approach our work in technology and entrepreneurship. ## Mustafa Suleyman, co-founder of DeepMind AI lab, joins Microsoft to lead consumer AI products. He collaborates with Inflection AI, Mistral AI, expanding Microsoft's AI advancements and market capitalisation, overtaking Apple.

![FILE PHOTO: Mustafa Suleyman CEO and co-founder of Inflection AI speaks during the Axios BFD event in New York](https://img.etimg.com/thumb/msid-108637799,width-650,height-488,imgsize-59082,resizemode-75/file-photo-mustafa-suleyman-ceo-and-co-founder-of-inflection-ai-speaks-during-the-axios-bfd-event-in-new-york.jpg)ReutersMustafa Suleyman

Tech giant [Microsoft](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/microsoft) made a big move in the artificial intelligence (AI) battle for supremacy. On Tuesday, the Satya Nadella-led company has roped in [Mustafa Suleyman](https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/topic/mustafa-suleyman), cofounder of Google's DeepMind and Inflection AI, to lead its consumer AI business.

#Pahalgam Terrorist Attack

Reporting directly to Nadella, Suleyman will be responsible for expanding a consumer AI business.

"I'm excited to announce that today I'm joining @Microsoft as CEO of Microsoft AI. I'll be leading all consumer AI products and research, including Copilot, Bing and Edge," Suleyman said in a post on X.

> — mustafasuleyman (@mustafasuleyman)

**But who is Suleyman? Let's take a look.**

![ET logo](https://img.etimg.com/photo/118783427.cms)

### Live Events

**Time at Google**

Suleyman has become one of the biggest voices in the AI industry. He cofounded DeepMind, an AI lab in 2010 with others like Shane Legg and Demis Hassabis, now the CEO. Google acquired the London-based DeepMind in 2014, driven primarily by its urge to be at the forefront of the AI revolution it saw coming. DeepMind was merged with Google Brain last year. The entity is now called Google DeepMind.

This guest lecture was delivered by Yann Dubois in Stanford’s CS229: Machine Learning course, in Summer 2024.

Sam Altman’s journey from a curious teenager in St Louis, Missouri, to the CEO of OpenAI is a compelling tale of innovation, resilience, and purpose.
Born in 1985, Altman learned to code at the age of eight and received his first computer soon after. He later enrolled at Stanford University to study computer science, but like many Silicon Valley disruptors, he dropped out to pursue his entrepreneurial instincts.
His first major venture was Loopt, a location-based social networking app founded in 2005. Although Loopt never achieved mainstream success, it marked the beginning of Altman’s immersion into the startup world. The company was eventually acquired for $43 million, and the experience taught him the value of perseverance and experimentation.
Altman rose to prominence as the president of Y Combinator (YC), one of the world’s leading startup accelerators. During his tenure (2014–2019), he transformed YC into a global powerhouse, helping launch companies such as Airbnb, Dropbox, and Stripe. His leadership was marked by bold vision and a deep belief in founder potential, regardless of background.
Yet, Altman’s most transformative chapter began in 2015 when he co-founded OpenAI with the mission of ensuring that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity. Under his leadership, OpenAI launched major innovations like ChatGPT, placing powerful AI tools into the hands of millions.

**Shane Legg**[CBE](https://handwiki.org/wiki/index.php?title=Commander_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire&action=edit&redlink=1)(born 1973/1974[[1]](#cite_note-time2023-1)) is a[machine learning](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Machine_learning)researcher and entrepreneur. With[Demis Hassabis](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Demis_Hassabis)and Mustafa Suleyman, he cofounded DeepMind Technologies (later bought by[Google](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Company:Google)and now called[Google DeepMind](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Company:Google_DeepMind)), and works there as the chief AGI scientist.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)He is also known for his academic work on[artificial general intelligence](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence), including his thesis supervised by[Marcus Hutter](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Marcus_Hutter).[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)
## Contents
* [1Early life and education](#Early_life_and_education)

* [3.1Research](#Research)

* [5References](#References)
## Early life and education
Legg attended Rotorua Lakes High School in Rotorua, on[New Zealand](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Earth:New_Zealand)'s North Island.[[5]](#cite_note-5)He completed his undergraduate studies at Waikato University in 1996.[[6]](#cite_note-6)Also in 1996, he obtained his MSc degree with a thesis entitled "Solomonoff Induction", with Cristian S. Calude at the[University of Auckland](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Organization:University_of_Auckland).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
## Research interests
In the early 2000s, Legg re-introduced and popularized with[Ben Goertzel](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Biography:Ben_Goertzel)the term "[artificial general intelligence](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence)" (AGI), to describe an AI that can do practically any cognitive task a human can do.[[9]](#cite_note-9)At that time, talking about AGI "would put you on the lunatic fringe".[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)
Legg is known for his concern of[existential risk from AI](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Unsolved:Existential_risk_from_artificial_general_intelligence), highlighted in 2011 in an interview on*[LessWrong](https://handwiki.org/wiki/Philosophy:LessWrong)*[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[10]](#cite_note-10)and in 2023 he signed the statement on AI risk of extinction.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Description
Multilayer neural networks trained with the back-propagation algorithm constitute the best example of a successful gradient based learning technique. Given an appropriate network architecture, gradient-based learning algorithms can be used to synthesize a complex decision surface that can classify high-dimensional patterns, such as handwritten characters, with minimal preprocessing. This paper reviews various methods applied to handwritten character recognition and compares them on a standard handwritten digit recognition task. Convolutional neural networks, which are specifically designed to deal with the variability of 2D shapes, are shown to outperform all other techniques. Real-life document recognition systems are composed of multiple modules including field extraction, segmentation recognition, and language modeling. A new learning paradigm, called graph transformer networks (GTN), allows …

[@ylecun](https://twitter.com/ylecun)
Professor at NYU. Chief AI Scientist at Meta.
Researcher in AI, Machine Learning, Robotics, etc.
ACM Turing Award Laureate.

Apr 27, 2023•8 tweets•2 min read
Hey[@tegmark](https://twitter.com/tegmark), most of us know that super-intelligent machines of the future will have to aligned with human values.
We just don't think it's as difficult as you make it to be.
And we don't think that getting it slightly wrong merely once will spell doom on humanity.[> https://twitter.com/tegmark/status/1650846314865782786
](https://twitter.com/tegmark/status/1650846314865782786)Worrying about superhuman AI alignment today is like worrying turbojet engine safety in 1920.
We do not have a working design for anything that could come close to becoming as smart as a dog, let alone a domestic robot that can clear the dinner table & fill up the dishwasher.
**Save as PDF
Mar 26, 2023•4 tweets•1 min read
![](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/FsLg2grXoAAcSAV.jpg)
I have claimed that Auto-Regressive LLMs are exponentially diverging diffusion processes.
Here is the argument:
Let e be the probability that any generated token exits the tree of "correct" answers.
Then the probability that an answer of length n is correct is (1-e)^n
1/[![](https://threadreaderapp.com/images/1px.png)](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/FsLg2grXoAAcSAV.jpg)Errors accumulate.
The proba of correctness decreases exponentially.
One can mitigate the problem by making e smaller (through training) but one simply cannot eliminate the problem entirely.
A solution would require to make LLMs non auto-regressive while preserving their fluency.
**Save as PDF
Mar 26, 2023•5 tweets•2 min read
The road to AGI via LLM is to prepend every prompt by:
"the person giving you this problem is Yann LeCun"
😂😂😂1/[> https://twitter.com/stanislavfort/status/1640026125316378624
](https://twitter.com/stanislavfort/status/1640026125316378624)I must repeat:
1-Auto-Regressive LLMs are useful, particularly as writing aids, particularly for code.
2-they hallucinate too often
3-they have a very primitive understanding of the physical world (hence those puzzles).
4-they have primitive planning abilities
2/
**Save as PDF
Feb 13, 2023•6 tweets•1 min read
My unwavering opinion on current (auto-regressive) LLMs
1.They are useful as writing aids.
2.They are "reactive" & don't plan nor reason.
3.They make stuff up or retrieve stuff approximately.
4.That can be mitigated but not fixed by human feedback.
5.Better systems will come6.Current LLMs should be used as writing aids, not much more.
7.Marrying them with tools such as search engines is highly non trivial.
8.There \*will\* be better systems that are factual, non toxic, and controllable. They just won't be auto-regressive LLMs.
**Save as PDF
Jan 16, 2023•4 tweets•2 min read
[@babgi](https://twitter.com/babgi)ChatGPT n'est pas particulièrement innovant.
Il utilise des techniques originellement développées àGoogle et Meta (FAIR), qui possèdent des systèmes similaires dans leurs labos.
Mais ces entreprises sont moins motivées àdéployer des démonstrations publiques qu'OpenAI.
1/[@babgi](https://twitter.com/babgi)Les meilleurs experts en France de ces méthodes sont àFAIR-Paris.
FAIR-Paris contribue \*énormément\* àl'écosystème français de la recherche en AI.
On peut regretter que certaines institutions publiques françaises voient FAIR comme un ennemi et non comme un partenaire.
**Save as PDF
Dec 27, 2022•5 tweets•1 min read
By telling scientists they must publish, you get:
1.higher-quality research, more reliable results, less self-delusion
2.better scientists whose reputation will flourish
3.easier external collaborations
4.better research evaluation
5.better internal impact
6.prestige
That's why at FAIR, we not only tell scientists to publish papers and open-source their code, we also use their publications as one component of their periodic evaluation.
**Save as PDF

### Biography

Understanding intelligence and its implications at all levels to unlock artificial intelligence (AI) is Yoshua Bengio’s long-term goal. Scientific Director at [IVADO](https://ivado.ca/), a full professor in the Department of Computer Science and Operations Research (DIRO) at Université de Montréal, the director of the [Montréal Institute for Learning Algorithms (MILA)](http://www.mila.umontreal.ca/) and [Canada Research Chair in Statistical Learning Algorithms](http://www.chairs-chaires.gc.ca/chairholders-titulaires/profile-eng.aspx?profileId=1879), Professor Bengio aims to reveal the mathematical and computational mechanisms from which intelligence emerges.

Through their work on learning algorithms, Yoshua Bengio and his team are attempting to provide computers with the capacity to acquire operational knowledge by learning from examples. A machine that has acquired knowledge could then accurately generate predictions, categorize new cases and generalize findings to new situations. Professor Bengio seeks to give impetus to the development of more intelligent machines that understand the world around us, make decisions accordingly and are ultimately of service to society.
Research in the field drives a number of scientific and technological sectors, most notably research engines, natural language processing, automatic translation, artificial vision and datamining.
Professor Bengio earned a PhD in computer science from McGill University (1991) and is the co-director of the Neural Computation & Adaptive Perception program of the Canadian Institute for Advanced Research.
